learningPromise: A self-contained component catalog for the Mermaid course renderer.
diagram:
graph LR
  C[Concept] --> S[Stacked code]
  S --> P[Split code]
  P --> T[Stepped code]
  T --> B[Storyboard]
  B --> Z[Zoomable diagram]

units.title: Concept Unit
units.body: Concept units are quiet prose blocks for setting context before a visual or code-heavy interaction. This fixture keeps the paragraph long enough to exercise typography, line height, and width without turning into a real lesson.

units.title: Stacked Code Walk
units.explanation: A stacked code-walk keeps the code and explanation in the normal page flow. Use it when one compact explanation is enough and there is no need for synchronized scrolling.

units.title: Split Code Walk
units.explanation: Split code-walks put the code on the left and scrollable explanation cards on the right. Lines 1-2: live profile data determines which commands exist. Line 6: only the first three commands receive keyboard shortcuts. Lines 11-13: navigation commands live beside generated commands, so the palette can show one merged list.

units.title: Stepped Code Walk

steps.beat: Start with the dispatcher. It owns the decision about which renderer handles the unit.
Concepts become prose-first sections with no interactive state.
Code-walks are where line highlighting and scroll sync start to matter.
Storyboards hand off to the Cinema Strip player.

units.question: If the right column scrolls, which code lines should stay highlighted?

reveal.explanation: The active card is the source of truth. The code panel simply mirrors its line list.

units.title: Compare Unit
units.lesson: Compare units make a contrast visible. The code blocks share the same numbered renderer as code-walks, so line treatment stays consistent.

units.title: Zoomable Diagram
units.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  A[Source data] --> B[Renderer]
  B --> C[Component]
  C --> D[Interaction]
  D --> E[E2E selector]
units.caption: A diagram story exercises Mermaid rendering, active-node contrast, and the zoom overlay controls.

units.title: Storyboard Unit
units.caption: Cinema Strip with scene tabs, Mermaid focus, explanation text, and optional code notes.

scenes.name: Collect
scenes.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  A[Fixture data] --> B[PAGE object]
  B --> C[Unit list]
scenes.explanation: The story page starts with fixed fixture data so every run exercises the same states.

highlights.note: The fixture names each unit kind explicitly.
Code-walks cover the interactive renderer path.

scenes.name: Render
scenes.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  A[Unit list] --> B[renderUnit]
  B --> C[DOM stories]
  C --> D[data-story-id]
scenes.explanation: The renderer emits stable story ids that e2e tests can locate without depending on text.

highlights.note: Story ids are optional but stable when provided.
The section becomes the e2e anchor.

units.title: Surprise Unit
units.body: The story page is also a contract. When a component changes, the fixture should make that change obvious before it leaks into generated course pages.
Use this page as the renderer's living component inventory. It is intentionally small, self-contained, and selector-friendly, so manual review and e2e coverage can share the same target.
